findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is an ill-defined, heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue mass centered within the pharyngeal mucosal space, specifically arising from the fossa of Rosenmüller in the nasopharynx. The mass causes mucosal asymmetry and often obliterates the adjacent parapharyngeal fat planes.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include unilateral mastoid effusion or middle ear fluid secondary to eustachian tube obstruction by the mass. Additionally, there may be enlarged, necrotic cervical lymph nodes, particularly in the retropharyngeal and level II stations, indicating regional metastatic spread.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of striated tonsillar enhancement to suggest tonsillitis, nor is there a thick rim-enhancing fluid collection to indicate a frank abscess. Furthermore, there is no hyperintense T1/T2 midline cystic lesion to suggest a benign Thornwaldt cyst.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an asymmetric, heterogeneously enhancing mass in the fossa of Rosenmüller with associated ipsilateral mastoid effusion and necrotic cervical lymphadenopathy.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Nasopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, given the origin in the fossa of Rosenmüller, local soft tissue invasion, and characteristic necrotic nodal metastases. This is the most common primary malignancy of the nasopharynx and often presents late with eustachian tube obstruction or palpable cervical lymphadenopathy.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Lymphoma, however it typically presents with massive, bulky, non-necrotic lymphadenopathy and homogeneous soft tissue thickening, without the prominent destructive local invasion or central nodal necrosis seen in this case.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Infection/abscess, given the serious risk of contiguous spread of infection from the retropharyngeal and parapharyngeal spaces into the mediastinum if left untreated. However, the absence of a distinct fluid collection with a thick enhancing rim and marked inflammatory fat stranding makes an acute pyogenic abscess highly unlikely.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately locally stage the tumor, paying particular attention to skull base bone erosion, intracranial extension, and perineural spread.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the chronicity of the mastoid effusion or to see if subtle mucosal asymmetry was previously overlooked.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the head and neck, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating soft tissue tumor margins, detecting subtle perineural tumor spread, and assessing for bone marrow infiltration at the skull base.
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences with fat saturation to precisely delineate the tumor extent and perineural spread, alongside T2-weighted sequences to differentiate the solid tumor from retained inflammatory middle ear fluid.
management.nuclearMedicine: An 18F-FDG PET-CT scan would be highly beneficial to stage the disease systemically, identify distant metastases, and detect occult nodal involvement prior to radical radiotherapy.
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis is needed, I would specify an endoscopic biopsy of the nasopharyngeal mucosal mass by the ENT surgeons to obtain histological confirmation.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Head and Neck Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact extent of parapharyngeal extension, skull base erosion, intracranial involvement, and the precise cervical lymph node stations involved to guide definitive chemoradiotherapy.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is an ill-defined, heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue mass centered within the pharyngeal mucosal space, specifically arising from the fossa of Rosenmüller in the nasopharynx. The mass causes mucosal asymmetry and often obliterates the adjacent parapharyngeal fat planes. Supporting features include unilateral mastoid effusion or middle ear fluid secondary to eustachian tube obstruction by the mass. Additionally, there may be enlarged, necrotic cervical lymph nodes, particularly in the retropharyngeal and level II stations, indicating regional metastatic spread. Importantly, there is no evidence of striated tonsillar enhancement to suggest tonsillitis, nor is there a thick rim-enhancing fluid collection to indicate a frank abscess. Furthermore, there is no hyperintense T1/T2 midline cystic lesion to suggest a benign Thornwaldt cyst. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of an asymmetric, heterogeneously enhancing mass in the fossa of Rosenmüller with associated ipsilateral mastoid effusion and necrotic cervical lymphadenopathy.

The appearances are most in keeping with Nasopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, given the origin in the fossa of Rosenmüller, local soft tissue invasion, and characteristic necrotic nodal metastases. This is the most common primary malignancy of the nasopharynx and often presents late with eustachian tube obstruction or palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. The principle differential would be Lymphoma, however it typically presents with massive, bulky, non-necrotic lymphadenopathy and homogeneous soft tissue thickening, without the prominent destructive local invasion or central nodal necrosis seen in this case. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Infection/abscess, given the serious risk of contiguous spread of infection from the retropharyngeal and parapharyngeal spaces into the mediastinum if left untreated. However, the absence of a distinct fluid collection with a thick enhancing rim and marked inflammatory fat stranding makes an acute pyogenic abscess highly unlikely.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to accurately locally stage the tumor, paying particular attention to skull base bone erosion, intracranial extension, and perineural spread. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to determine the chronicity of the mastoid effusion or to see if subtle mucosal asymmetry was previously overlooked. I would recommend a dedicated MRI of the head and neck, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating soft tissue tumor margins, detecting subtle perineural tumor spread, and assessing for bone marrow infiltration at the skull base. I would specify T1-weighted pre- and post-contrast sequences with fat saturation to precisely delineate the tumor extent and perineural spread, alongside T2-weighted sequences to differentiate the solid tumor from retained inflammatory middle ear fluid. An 18F-FDG PET-CT scan would be highly beneficial to stage the disease systemically, identify distant metastases, and detect occult nodal involvement prior to radical radiotherapy. If tissue diagnosis is needed, I would specify an endoscopic biopsy of the nasopharyngeal mucosal mass by the ENT surgeons to obtain histological confirmation. This should go to the Head and Neck Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the exact extent of parapharyngeal extension, skull base erosion, intracranial involvement, and the precise cervical lymph node stations involved to guide definitive chemoradiotherapy.
